sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 40-year-old male, presented with symptoms of shortness of breath, which he describes as catching his breath a lot more than usual, and experiencing sudden onset of shortness of breath without a clear reason.
He reports that he considers himself to be pretty healthy and has noticed this symptom for some time.
He also mentions that his eyes water, but attributes it to allergies.
He has a dog and gets eyebugers, and mentions that the last time he experienced shortness of breath, he was given a nebulizer at urgent care and prescribed an inhaler, which he uses as needed.
He denies any rash, nausea, vomiting, lip or throat swelling, or issues with heavy drinking.
He reports diarrhea after heavy drinking, but no other issues with allergies in the past.
He has noticed symptoms in various situations, including at home, outside, and in the presence of his cat.
He has not had any recent travels and is currently asymptomatic.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse is okay, and his pulse ox is normal.
Physical exam: The patient appears to be a little bit nervous, but does not appear to be in any distress.
There is no rash or angioedema, and no audible stridor.
The lungs exam reveals faint expiratory wheezing bilaterally in all lung fields.
Test Results: The chest x-ray and pulmonary function test were both normal.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has newly diagnosed allergic asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; Active; Allergies; Unknown; Unknown.
Plan:
I prescribed albuterol inhaler to be used as needed for symptoms.
I also prescribed Singulair 10mg once daily to decrease the occurrences of asthma symptoms.
I will proceed with allergy testing, starting with skin testing to identify potential triggers.
We will schedule the skin testing for next week.
Follow-up: The patient is scheduled to return next week for skin testing.
